What convinced the board was the track record.

The reason we recommend it is that the upside is real.
Izabel, I read your memo. The case is strong, but let me push on it. What exactly makes this deal worth doing?
What makes it compelling is speed. It is their proprietary technology that puts us in the medical market a year sooner. That is the bottom line.
Fair. But the merits do not answer the price. Is it not too expensive?
It is the valuation, not the idea, that worries the CFO too. That objection is fair, so we will substantiate every number with an independent audit. We will not take their figures at face value.
Some on the board assume a bigger rival will just copy the technology.
That assertion rests on a bias toward size. What the record shows is the opposite: it was reliability, not scale, that won our own clients. I can rebut that with our track record.
And the main caveat?

Integration. What could slow both teams is a clash of engineering cultures. But the reason I still recommend it is that the upside is real and the downside is manageable. The case is close to watertight.
What makes the case compelling is speed.
The bottom line is that we would gain a year.
What the record shows is that reliability wins.

Izabel, it is Nathan from the board, with my read on your memo before we vote. Let me be direct about what convinced me. What makes the case compelling is not the excitement; it is the logic. It was the speed argument that landed hardest for me: acquiring the technology puts us in the medical market a year sooner than building it ourselves. That is the bottom line. I also liked that you did not hide the objections. The main one is price, and you were right not to take their revenue figures at face value; an independent audit will substantiate them. There is one caveat I want on the record. What worries me is integration, because a clash of engineering cultures could slow both teams. But the reason I will vote yes is that the merits outweigh the risks. The case, honestly, is close to watertight. Call me before the meeting.

Good morning, Izabel. It is Claire, your analyst, with a quick note on how the board will read the memo. My job is to separate fact from opinion, so let me flag both. The facts are solid. It is the proprietary technology that gives Nordic Sense its edge, and that is documented. But watch one assertion. The claim that a bigger rival will simply copy the design rests on a bias toward size, and I would rebut it directly. What the record actually shows is the opposite: it was reliability, not scale, that won your own clients. My advice is to lead with the strongest counterargument, not to bury it. What persuades a sceptical board is a case that answers its own objections. The reason your memo works is that it does exactly that. Keep the caveat about integration visible and honest. Talk soon.
